item.title: I stopped treating old phones like e-waste and started treating them like tiny servers
item.itunes:title: I stopped treating old phones like e-waste and started treating them like tiny servers
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I've always had more phones than I actually needed. That's partly because of the profession I'm in, and most of those phones have spent years sitting in a drawer. I only use one phone daily, but after years of tinkering with home servers and self-hosted services, I started wondering why not use these old phones for the same purpose?

Even if your phone is five years old, it still packs more computing power than many entry-level NAS devices. Take my NAS, for example, the Ugreen DH2300. It's a capable entry-level NAS, but it's powered by a Rockchip processor, comes with 4GB of RAM, and is primarily designed for storage rather than raw performance.

Compare that to a mid-range Android phone from 2020. Chances are it has more RAM, a significantly faster processor, and better overall hardware. Of course, there are limitations. Battery health becomes a concern when a phone is plugged in around the clock, and phones weren't designed to run like traditional NAS systems. But despite those drawbacks, they're surprisingly useful as tiny servers. Over the past few months, I've repurposed a few of my old phones into dedicated servers. One handles media streaming, while another acts as a private VPN endpoint using Tailscale, and they’ve been very reliable.

I couldn’t get Jellyfin remote streaming working until I found this app

Setting up Tailscale was way quicker and easier than I expected.

1
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Turning my phone into a media server

It's a lot easier than you think

The easiest thing you can utilize an old phone for is a media server because it doesn't take a lot. My starting point was the iQOO 12, which I'll probably not call an old phone because it was announced only three years back, and it's also probably overkill for this project, but in the best way possible. This Android phone has a Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 processor, 16GB of RAM, and 512GB of storage, and the battery is also quite decent at 5000 mAh. The phone has enough headroom to run a lightweight media setup without immediately choking on it.

The first step in turning a phone into a media server is installing Termux. I used it to install the Jellyfin server package available in the Termux package repository and then pointed Jellyfin to the folder on the phone where I keep my media files. The setup process is fairly straightforward. You need to install the server, launch it, complete the initial setup wizard, and add your media library. Once that's done, the phone acts as the server, while other devices simply connect to it as clients.

Once the library was in place, I kept everything local. Jellyfin runs over a standard web interface, so all I had to do was find the phone's local IP address and open it from another device on the same Wi-Fi network.

Your old phone can double into a dedicated Tailscale node

And it works perfectly fine

The second old phone ended up becoming something even more useful than the media server. I wanted a way to securely access my home network when I was away, but I did not want to deal with port forwarding, dynamic DNS services, or exposing self-hosted apps directly to the internet. So I turned one of my spare phones into a dedicated Tailscale node.

I installed Tailscale on the phone, signed in with my account, approved the device on my tailnet, and then enabled Android's Always-on VPN setting, so the connection would automatically come back after reboots. Once the phone was permanently connected to Wi-Fi and power, it essentially became a fixed point inside my home network. Tailscale assigns every connected device its own private address and lets them communicate over an encrypted mesh network, so the phone immediately became reachable from my laptop, tablet, and other devices wherever I was.

I took things a step further by configuring the phone as an exit node. Tailscale supports running exit nodes on Android, which allows another device to route its traffic through the phone remotely. After enabling the option inside the Tailscale app and approving it from the Tailscale admin console, I could connect from outside my home and have my traffic appear as though it was coming from my home network.

Don't rely too much on your phone server

Because phones are never supposed to do this

While you can definitely use old phones as tiny servers, they are not a replacement for a NAS. A NAS is purpose built for this job. It has the hardware, software, and reliability features needed to store data safely and run services around the clock.

Deals

Score Phone Deals and Savings on Phones & Mobile Gear

Discover discounts and limited-time offers on phones, chargers, battery packs, and mobile accessories to make repurposing or upgrading devices affordable. Browse phone deals for budget handsets, power solutions, and networking add-ons to save.
Deals Explore Phones & Mobile Deals

The biggest advantage of a NAS is that it isn't dependent on a battery. As long as it's connected to power, you don't have to worry about it shutting down unexpectedly. Phones, on the other hand, were never designed to stay plugged in 24/7. Leave one charging continuously for months, and you're likely to run into battery degradation, heat-related issues, and more. While it's fun to experiment with different server setups on an old phone, I don't see it as a permanent solution.

That said, some use cases make more sense than others. I can see a Jellyfin media server working reasonably well on a phone because it doesn't necessarily need to run all day. You can power it up when needed, stream your content, and put it away afterward. But if you want a device that remains online all the time and acts as a reliable gateway into your home network, you're probably better off using a NAS, a mini PC, or another device designed for this kind of operation.

There's also the software side of things. Most self-hosting tools and server applications are built with Linux servers, NAS devices, and desktop operating systems in mind. Android support is often an afterthought. While apps such as Tailscale work surprisingly well on Android, the experience is not always as polished or as capable as it is on a dedicated Linux machine. Tailscale itself notes that Android exit nodes are less optimized than Linux-based ones and rely on user-space routing, which means performance won't match a dedicated server.

I turned an old Android phone into a home server, and it’s more useful than my Raspberry Pi

Don't let it collect dust in a drawer.

34
By  Jasmine Mannan

item.title: I use Claude Pro, Qwen 3-Coder, and Gemma 4 together, and it's the most cost-efficient AI workflow I've ever built
item.itunes:title: I use Claude Pro, Qwen 3-Coder, and Gemma 4 together, and it's the most cost-efficient AI workflow I've ever built
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
AI has found its way into almost every workflow imaginable. Large language and image models have become nearly inseparable from creative, programming, and research-based work, and for many professionals, the productivity enhancement is so compelling that they're no longer optional to use. It's also true that the best flagship models from Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic are among the most expensive to run, and using them on a daily basis to drive productivity quickly becomes an exercise in resource management.

That said, there are some very capable open-source models available today at no cost, and while some users have managed to replace their paid subscriptions entirely, I've found that going fully open-source comes with a sour set of compromises. The sweet spot, in my experience, lies in a hybrid approach. Here's how I'm pairing a premium model with two open-source ones, and why the combination works better than using either alone.

My local LLM can call Claude when it's stuck, and it changed everything about my local-first setup

Local LLMs aren't very good on their own

14
By  Anurag Singh

What each model does in the workflow

The frontier model, the coder, and the generative workhorse

If you've read my earlier commentary on hybrid LLM workflows, you'll know that I've been a vocal advocate for pairing a premium model with a locally hosted one. What I've since discovered, is the fact that two models weren't quite enough. The missing piece, at least for my workflow, was a dedicated coding model, and that's where Qwen 3-Coder 30B enters the picture.

The "division of labor" is quite simple to understand, especially since it's built around each model's individual strengths. Claude Pro, naturally, remains the premium anchor, which is reserved for the tasks that demand frontier-level reasoning and the platform-exclusive features (such as interactive visuals and artifacts) that I rely on. Qwen 3-Coder takes over the coding lane, handling the iterative code-generation cycles, boilerplate, and the back-and-forth debugging that would otherwise eat through my Claude allowance.

Gemma 4 24B is tasked with other generative tasks, such as first drafts, summarization, brainstorming, and everything that comes in between. At this point, you might wonder why I'm not using ChatGPT for this. The answer really is quite simple, and it is the fact that Gemma 4 runs on the same local Ollama interface as Qwen 3-Coder, which means both open-source models operate under one unified workflow. Amongst the three, there's almost no redundancy, which means every model is operating in the lane it is best suited for.

How the three models work together

Less like multitasking, more like a relay race

The best way to illustrate how this workflow operates is to walk through what a typical session looks like. If I'm building a Python utility from the ground up, the first place to start is Gemma 4, where I will outline what the utility should do, manage baseline expectations, brainstorm the structure, and have it evaluate the constraints and opportunities surrounding the idea. Gemma 4 24B is fast, responsive, and lighter than the 31B model, which makes it perfectly capable of producing a working first draft that I can evaluate and build on.

Qwen 3-Coder comes next, and enters the project in the iterative phase. This includes generating the code, adding features, testing, and debugging. This is precisely where the back-and-forth happens, and it's also the kind of workload that used to drain my 5-hour limit on Claude. Qwen handles it locally, and the fact that it runs on the same Ollama interface as Gemma 4 means the transition between the two is as seamless as switching gears in a car you're driving.

Claude enters the workflow at the very end as a "quality assurance" layer, and quite deliberately so, given its role in the workflow. Once the project is functional and needs a final pass, GUI enhancements, fixing a particularly stubborn bug that Qwen can't handle, or has a feature that could benefit from interactive visuals, it's time to use the reserved tokens on Claude.

The setup does have its trade-offs

But the ones it has are quite manageable

The most common pushback I've received when detailing this approach is the fact that it demands capable hardware, and that's quite fair. Running Qwen-3 Coder 30B and Gemma 4 locally means you will, at the very least, require 16GB of VRAM to keep the generation speeds comfortable. While the models are free to run, the GPU isn't, and that's a cost that's worth accounting for.

There's also a pertinent question of context-transfer. For smaller, lightweight utilities, passing the baton between the three models is seamless, but as a codebase swells, each handoff means losing the conversational history and context that kept the momentum of the project growing. I've found that keeping a running project brief in a text file is beneficial for mitigating it, but that's also an extra step the workflow demands.

Another common critique is that the use of three models together is effectively overkill when Gemma 4 24B can handle light coding on its own. On some projects, it certainly is, and not every session will warrant the use of all three models at the same time. But when a coding task benefits from a purpose-built model, the difference in output quality between Qwen and Gemma handling the prompt is noticeable enough to justify the switch, and having both models already downloaded on the same interface means the cost of keeping that option available is effectively zero.

An economic model that only costs you a little effort

Regrettably enough, the most common reason cloud AI subscribers shy away from local models is a combination of perceived complexity along the set-up process (which still seems tedious to many), doubts about their capability, and hardware limitations that stand in the way. In reality, services such as Ollama have already reduced setup to just a few terminal commands, and lighter Mixture-of-Experts variants exist for both local models, meaning you don't need flagship hardware to get started. While neither of the models I have talked about will immediately replace your Claude subscription, if you use them alongside it, they'll make sure you're spending your tokens where they matter most, and perhaps more importantly, you'll not lose your momentum to a usage reset.

Claude

Claude is an AI assistant and LLM developed by Anthropic.

See at Claude

Qwen 3-Coder takes over the coding lane, handling the iterative code-generation cycles, boilerplate, and the back-and-forth debugging that would otherwise eat through my Claude allowance.

Gemma 4 24B is tasked with other generative tasks, such as first drafts, summarization, brainstorming, and everything that comes in between. At this point, you might wonder why I'm not using ChatGPT for this. The answer really is quite simple, and it is the fact that Gemma 4 runs on the same local Ollama interface as Qwen 3-Coder, which means both open-source models operate under one unified workflow. Amongst the three, there's almost no redundancy, which means every model is operating in the lane it is best suited for.

How the three models work together

Less like multitasking, more like a relay race

The best way to illustrate how this workflow operates is to walk through what a typical session looks like. If I'm building a Python utility from the ground up, the first place to start is Gemma 4, where I will outline what the utility should do, manage baseline expectations, brainstorm the structure, and have it evaluate the constraints and opportunities surrounding the idea. Gemma 4 24B is fast, responsive, and lighter than the 31B model, which makes it perfectly capable of producing a working first draft that I can evaluate and build on.

Qwen 3-Coder comes next, and enters the project in the iterative phase. This includes generating the code, adding features, testing, and debugging. This is precisely where the back-and-forth happens, and it's also the kind of workload that used to drain my 5-hour limit on Claude. Qwen handles it locally, and the fact that it runs on the same Ollama interface as Gemma 4 means the transition between the two is as seamless as switching gears in a car you're driving.

Claude enters the workflow at the very end as a "quality assurance" layer, and quite deliberately so, given its role in the workflow. Once the project is functional and needs a final pass, GUI enhancements, fixing a particularly stubborn bug that Qwen can't handle, or has a feature that could benefit from interactive visuals, it's time to use the reserved tokens on Claude.

The setup does have its trade-offs

But the ones it has are quite manageable

The most common pushback I've received when detailing this approach is the fact that it demands capable hardware, and that's quite fair. Running Qwen-3 Coder 30B and Gemma 4 locally means you will, at the very least, require 16GB of VRAM to keep the generation speeds comfortable. While the models are free to run, the GPU isn't, and that's a cost that's worth accounting for.

There's also a pertinent question of context-transfer. For smaller, lightweight utilities, passing the baton between the three models is seamless, but as a codebase swells, each handoff means losing the conversational history and context that kept the momentum of the project growing. I've found that keeping a running project brief in a text file is beneficial for mitigating it, but that's also an extra step the workflow demands.

Another common critique is that the use of three models together is effectively overkill when Gemma 4 24B can handle light coding on its own. On some projects, it certainly is, and not every session will warrant the use of all three models at the same time. But when a coding task benefits from a purpose-built model, the difference in output quality between Qwen and Gemma handling the prompt is noticeable enough to justify the switch, and having both models already downloaded on the same interface means the cost of keeping that option available is effectively zero.

An economic model that only costs you a little effort

Regrettably enough, the most common reason cloud AI subscribers shy away from local models is a combination of perceived complexity along the set-up process (which still seems tedious to many), doubts about their capability, and hardware limitations that stand in the way. In reality, services such as Ollama have already reduced setup to just a few terminal commands, and lighter Mixture-of-Experts variants exist for both local models, meaning you don't need flagship hardware to get started. While neither of the models I have talked about will immediately replace your Claude subscription, if you use them alongside it, they'll make sure you're spending your tokens where they matter most, and perhaps more importantly, you'll not lose your momentum to a usage reset.

Claude

Claude is an AI assistant and LLM developed by Anthropic.

See at Claude

item.title: Claude Code's /goal command finally makes 'agentic' mean something
item.itunes:title: Claude Code's /goal command finally makes 'agentic' mean something
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I've had a problem with the term "agentic" ever since AI companies started using it. It wasn't originally coined by them, but they've massively popularized it, and now it's lost most of its meaning, the way dozens of other buzzwords have. Claude Code is a great tool, but it just seemed like an AI that I could converse with from my terminal. I could also get it to make changes directly to files, instead of me copying and pasting from a web window. Pretty cool, but it felt underwhelming for what was marketed as an agentic tool.

Anthropic released the new /goal command for Claude Code, and it's the first time the word "agentic" actually felt appropriate. It lets the user define an end goal for Claude to reach. Then, Claude Code does its work and won't stop until the goal is met. If it doesn't meet the conditions for stopping, it'll just keep going. The process feels a lot different from constantly conversing with AI and trying to steer it toward your vision with each new prompt. Now Claude Code just takes the necessary steps and tells you when it's done.

Claude Code works best when you stop asking it to code

Claude Code became far more useful once I stopped treating it like a code generator and started using it to understand projects and terminal chaos.

11
By  Jeff Butts

What /goal actually does (and doesn't do)

One model builds and the other judges

The key feature of /goal is that it operates as a loop and will keep iterating until it achieves the result you specified. Each time Claude completes a turn, a separate model evaluates the work to determine whether the goal has been reached. If the answer is no, it gives Claude a brief reason that the desired conditions are not yet met, and the cycle repeats. On the other hand, if the model decides that the work satisfies the user's criteria, it passes control back to you. Having a different model to evaluate the work is an important part of the process because it helps keep things honest, since Claude isn't grading its own homework.

When I used /goal to fix a Node.js auth module, I didn't need to check in on it at all. I set the goal to have all npm tests passing, and zero lint errors. Once npm test and npm run lint come back with no warnings or errors, the goal should be considered achieved. Claude proceeded to write the code, run the tests, and react to the failures by correcting them and retesting. The evaluator's job is straightforward because it has concrete criteria to judge by: the tests either pass or they don't. Claude kept coding and testing for a few minutes until all tests came back green.

The setup that makes this work best

CLAUDE.md, auto mode, and a clear-cut goal

If you rush into using /goal without any forethought, you'll probably end up disappointed. It will technically function, but the results are much better if you take a few minutes to plan out your approach. The first thing you need is a well-defined goal — something that the model can easily test against. "Improve the code" isn't a good use of the command because the criteria is subjective and ambiguous. Focus on asking for measurable results that can be easily verified, without leaving much up to interpretation.

To avoid Claude making assumptions about your codebase, a CLAUDE.md file is essential. This is where you store info about your project, like the architecture decisions, coding conventions, and acceptance criteria. Claude will read the file automatically every time it takes a turn. It avoids the need to answer a bunch of clarifying questions, or worse, having Claude infer expectations about the code.

Auto mode is only necessary if you want to bypass the per-tool prompt within a turn. If you prefer that Claude prompt you when it needs to use a tool, then you don't need auto mode. Turning it on just gives you a more autonomous and unattended experience.

Let Claude cook

But don't leave the kitchen

We may finally have a truly agentic AI experience, but it also sounds a lot like we're just unleashing unsupervised AI on a codebase. I'd still recommend glancing at the terminal every now and then to make sure Claude Code isn't doing anything crazy, but as long as you have solid backups and a well-crafted CLAUDE.md file, there's not much to worry about.

Deals

AI and Developer Software Deals — Save on Subscriptions

Cut costs with top software and AI deals — discounts on developer tools, cloud credits, collaboration platforms, code editors, and subscription services. Browse Software, AI & Subscriptions offers to boost workflows and lock in savings today.
Deals Explore Software, AI & Subscriptions Deals

Every decision Claude makes during a /goal run is logged, including what it ran, what it changed, and why it moved to the next step. That's a better paper trail than I'd leave if I were doing all the coding myself. If something does go sideways, Ctrl+C will stop the run immediately. The risk isn't strictly zero, but it's not like AI is running amok on your system, either.

This feels like delegating instead of prompting

The introduction of the /goal command marks the first time where Claude Code actually felt like an agentic tool that wasn't just waiting for my next message. If you're still prompting turn by turn, try out /goal to see how much faster things move when you don't have to hold its hand.

Claude

Claude is an AI assistant and LLM developed by Anthropic.

See at Claude

item.title: 7 everyday items I no longer have to buy thanks to 3D printing
item.itunes:title: 7 everyday items I no longer have to buy thanks to 3D printing
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Once you start using a 3D printer for practical projects, it changes how you view everyday objects. Many items that once seemed like automatic purchases are now easy to create at home. Over time, I have found that printing these small things saves money and gives me exactly what I want. Here are seven everyday items I now 3D print instead of buying.

Cable management clips and organizers

Tidy up your workspace with custom solutions

Cable clutter used to be one of my biggest pet peeves. Store-bought clips never seemed to fit my setup, and adhesive models often failed after a few weeks. Once I started printing my own, everything changed. I was finally able to create clips that perfectly matched my needs.

I now print a wide variety of clips and trays. Some attach under my desk to route power cables, while others snap around thinner wires, such as USB or audio cables. It is easy to adjust dimensions so each clip holds cables snugly without damaging them. I even print multi-slot combs to organize groups of wires.

Being able to redesign and reprint clips as my workspace evolves is a significant advantage. When I add new gear or change monitor layouts, I can print updated clips in just a few hours. My workspace is much cleaner and more efficient thanks to these small but impactful prints.

Drawer dividers and organizers

Customize every drawer to your exact needs

Store-bought drawer organizers rarely fit my drawers well. Many were too short, too tall, or left wasted space. I finally tried printing custom dividers, and it made a huge difference. Now every drawer in my house can be organized exactly how I want.

I use parametric divider models that let me enter exact drawer dimensions. This way, the printed dividers fill the space without gaps or overlap. I can also customize compartment sizes based on what I plan to store, whether it is office supplies, kitchen tools, or bathroom items.

Another benefit is the ability to change things over time. As my storage needs evolve, I can print new dividers without having to buy a whole new organizer. It has helped me keep drawers tidy and made it easier to find what I need. Once you start printing these, it is hard to go back.

Replacement knobs and handles

Fix broken parts quickly and affordably

Broken knobs and handles used to be annoying to replace. Many were no longer available, and universal replacements rarely looked right. Now I just print them myself. It is one of the most practical uses I have found for my 3D printer.

I use PETG or ASA filament to ensure durability and heat resistance. I have printed replacement knobs for my oven, fan controls, and even for vintage electronics. If I cannot find an exact match online, it is usually easy to model a simple knob or handle myself.

Printing spares is another advantage. When I print a new knob, I often make two or three copies of it. That way, I always have a backup ready if another one breaks. This small habit has saved me a lot of time and hassle over the years.

Phone and tablet stands

Print stands for every device and location

I used to buy phone and tablet stands, but they were often overpriced or poorly designed. Once I started printing them, I never looked back. Now I have custom stands for every room and every device I use.

For my nightstand, I printed a low-profile phone stand with the perfect angle for viewing the screen. In the kitchen, I use a sturdy adjustable tablet holder to follow recipes. I also have a portable folding stand that fits easily into a travel bag, so I’m always prepared on the go.

Customization is the key benefit. I can adjust angles, add cable channels, and even print matching stands for different devices. Printing a few extras is inexpensive, so I always have the right stand ready no matter where I need to use my phone or tablet.

Shelf brackets and wall hooks

Strong, customizable supports for home projects

I once assumed 3D printed parts were not strong enough for shelving or wall hooks. After experimenting with materials and print settings, I discovered they can be very durable. Now I print shelf brackets and hooks for many areas of my home.

I use high infill settings and thicker walls when printing brackets or load-bearing hooks. PETG works well for most of these parts. I have printed floating shelf supports, decorative open brackets, and various hooks for coats, bags, and tools.

Flexibility is another advantage. If I decide to rearrange a room or update its look, I can easily print new brackets or hooks. It is a cost-effective way to add both function and style to my living spaces. The more I do it, the more I rely on these printed solutions.

Battery holders and dispensers

Keep your batteries organized and accessible

Batteries used to be one of the most significant clutter problems in my home. They rolled loose in drawers or sat half-open in original packaging. Printing custom battery holders solved this problem completely. Now I always know where my batteries are and which ones are charged.

I use gravity-fed dispensers for AA and AAA batteries. They make it easy to rotate stock and quickly grab fresh batteries. For larger cells, such as 18650s, I print separate holders with slots for both charged and depleted cells. It is simple but highly effective.

Subscribe to the newsletter for more practical 3D-print ideas

Looking for more ways to turn everyday fixes into 3D-printed solutions? Subscribe to the newsletter for broader coverage of practical projects, design tweaks, and material advice to expand your printing toolkit.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Having dedicated battery storage saves time and reduces clutter. I no longer waste energy hunting for batteries or wondering which ones still have a charge. It is a slight improvement, but it makes a big difference in keeping my home organized.

Measuring scoops and kitchen tools

Print kitchen gadgets tailored to your habits

Many small kitchen tools can be improved with 3D printing. I now print measuring scoops, funnels, bag clips, and other gadgets that perfectly fit my cooking style. For dry goods and baking ingredients, printed scoops are a handy tool.

I design scoops to fit inside the specific storage containers I use. That way, I can leave a scoop in each jar without the handle sticking out. I have also printed bag clips with integrated scoops, making it easy to measure pet food or pantry items quickly and accurately.

For food-contact items, I use food-safe PLA and spray them with a polyurethane sealant. Just to be safe, I also wash them by hand. With proper care, these tools can last a long time and perform just as well as commercial products. Being able to customize them adds convenience and helps me make the most of my kitchen space.

Practical prints make everyday life easier

The more I print useful items, the more value I get from my 3D printer. Each small project saves a bit of money and enhances the functionality of my home. Over time, those benefits add up. I now think twice before buying plastic organizers or gadgets. More often than not, printing what I need works better and is less expensive. If you are looking for practical ways to use your printer, these everyday items are a great place to start.

The more I print useful items, the more value I get from my 3D printer. Each small project saves a bit of money and enhances the functionality of my home. Over time, those benefits add up. I now think twice before buying plastic organizers or gadgets. More often than not, printing what I need works better and is less expensive. If you are looking for practical ways to use your printer, these everyday items are a great place to start.
item.content:encoded:
Once you start using a 3D printer for practical projects, it changes how you view everyday objects. Many items that once seemed like automatic purchases are now easy to create at home. Over time, I have found that printing these small things saves money and gives me exactly what I want. Here are seven everyday items I now 3D print instead of buying.

Cable management clips and organizers

Tidy up your workspace with custom solutions

Cable clutter used to be one of my biggest pet peeves. Store-bought clips never seemed to fit my setup, and adhesive models often failed after a few weeks. Once I started printing my own, everything changed. I was finally able to create clips that perfectly matched my needs.

I now print a wide variety of clips and trays. Some attach under my desk to route power cables, while others snap around thinner wires, such as USB or audio cables. It is easy to adjust dimensions so each clip holds cables snugly without damaging them. I even print multi-slot combs to organize groups of wires.

Being able to redesign and reprint clips as my workspace evolves is a significant advantage. When I add new gear or change monitor layouts, I can print updated clips in just a few hours. My workspace is much cleaner and more efficient thanks to these small but impactful prints.

Drawer dividers and organizers

Customize every drawer to your exact needs

Store-bought drawer organizers rarely fit my drawers well. Many were too short, too tall, or left wasted space. I finally tried printing custom dividers, and it made a huge difference. Now every drawer in my house can be organized exactly how I want.

I use parametric divider models that let me enter exact drawer dimensions. This way, the printed dividers fill the space without gaps or overlap. I can also customize compartment sizes based on what I plan to store, whether it is office supplies, kitchen tools, or bathroom items.

Another benefit is the ability to change things over time. As my storage needs evolve, I can print new dividers without having to buy a whole new organizer. It has helped me keep drawers tidy and made it easier to find what I need. Once you start printing these, it is hard to go back.

Replacement knobs and handles

Fix broken parts quickly and affordably

Broken knobs and handles used to be annoying to replace. Many were no longer available, and universal replacements rarely looked right. Now I just print them myself. It is one of the most practical uses I have found for my 3D printer.

I use PETG or ASA filament to ensure durability and heat resistance. I have printed replacement knobs for my oven, fan controls, and even for vintage electronics. If I cannot find an exact match online, it is usually easy to model a simple knob or handle myself.

Printing spares is another advantage. When I print a new knob, I often make two or three copies of it. That way, I always have a backup ready if another one breaks. This small habit has saved me a lot of time and hassle over the years.

Phone and tablet stands

Print stands for every device and location

I used to buy phone and tablet stands, but they were often overpriced or poorly designed. Once I started printing them, I never looked back. Now I have custom stands for every room and every device I use.

For my nightstand, I printed a low-profile phone stand with the perfect angle for viewing the screen. In the kitchen, I use a sturdy adjustable tablet holder to follow recipes. I also have a portable folding stand that fits easily into a travel bag, so I’m always prepared on the go.

Customization is the key benefit. I can adjust angles, add cable channels, and even print matching stands for different devices. Printing a few extras is inexpensive, so I always have the right stand ready no matter where I need to use my phone or tablet.

Shelf brackets and wall hooks

Strong, customizable supports for home projects

I once assumed 3D printed parts were not strong enough for shelving or wall hooks. After experimenting with materials and print settings, I discovered they can be very durable. Now I print shelf brackets and hooks for many areas of my home.

I use high infill settings and thicker walls when printing brackets or load-bearing hooks. PETG works well for most of these parts. I have printed floating shelf supports, decorative open brackets, and various hooks for coats, bags, and tools.

Flexibility is another advantage. If I decide to rearrange a room or update its look, I can easily print new brackets or hooks. It is a cost-effective way to add both function and style to my living spaces. The more I do it, the more I rely on these printed solutions.

Battery holders and dispensers

Keep your batteries organized and accessible

Batteries used to be one of the most significant clutter problems in my home. They rolled loose in drawers or sat half-open in original packaging. Printing custom battery holders solved this problem completely. Now I always know where my batteries are and which ones are charged.

I use gravity-fed dispensers for AA and AAA batteries. They make it easy to rotate stock and quickly grab fresh batteries. For larger cells, such as 18650s, I print separate holders with slots for both charged and depleted cells. It is simple but highly effective.

Subscribe to the newsletter for more practical 3D-print ideas

Looking for more ways to turn everyday fixes into 3D-printed solutions? Subscribe to the newsletter for broader coverage of practical projects, design tweaks, and material advice to expand your printing toolkit.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Having dedicated battery storage saves time and reduces clutter. I no longer waste energy hunting for batteries or wondering which ones still have a charge. It is a slight improvement, but it makes a big difference in keeping my home organized.

Measuring scoops and kitchen tools

Print kitchen gadgets tailored to your habits

Many small kitchen tools can be improved with 3D printing. I now print measuring scoops, funnels, bag clips, and other gadgets that perfectly fit my cooking style. For dry goods and baking ingredients, printed scoops are a handy tool.

I design scoops to fit inside the specific storage containers I use. That way, I can leave a scoop in each jar without the handle sticking out. I have also printed bag clips with integrated scoops, making it easy to measure pet food or pantry items quickly and accurately.

For food-contact items, I use food-safe PLA and spray them with a polyurethane sealant. Just to be safe, I also wash them by hand. With proper care, these tools can last a long time and perform just as well as commercial products. Being able to customize them adds convenience and helps me make the most of my kitchen space.

Practical prints make everyday life easier

The more I print useful items, the more value I get from my 3D printer. Each small project saves a bit of money and enhances the functionality of my home. Over time, those benefits add up. I now think twice before buying plastic organizers or gadgets. More often than not, printing what I need works better and is less expensive. If you are looking for practical ways to use your printer, these everyday items are a great place to start.

item.title: PowerToys is Microsoft's test lab for the Windows features you actually want, and some have actually made it over
item.itunes:title: PowerToys is Microsoft's test lab for the Windows features you actually want, and some have actually made it over
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Over the years, there have been plenty of features requested for Microsoft to add to Windows. Sometimes, the company listens, like how it brought back taskbar ungrouping on Windows 11 after the initial release removed it, or how it added a package manager to Windows 10 with Winget. But most of the time, it feels like the company takes ages to catch up with popular requests.

And lately, it looks like Microsoft is relying on a separate tool, PowerToys, as a testing ground for all the features users actually to see in Windows. I'd go as far as to say PowerToys is indispensable if you have a Windows PC, and sometimes, some of the great features in it eventually get built into Windows in some capacity.

It started with window management

FancyZones may have inspired Windows 11

I can't claim with total confidence that these two are entirely related, but you could definitely argue that the inclusion of FancyZones in PowerToys has some connection to Windows 11's Snap Layouts feature. FancyZones was part of the "initial" release of PowerToys for Windows 10 back in 2019, and it's hard not to see how it might have inspired Windows 11 when it debuted two years later.

Snap Layouts was one of the headlining features of Windows 11, and while it doesn't have flexibility and ease of use of FancyZones (which is still available in PowerToys today), it does bring some of that idea straight into the operating system. Now, you can snap apps side by side using all kinds of suggested layouts, not just a basic split into quadrants like Windows 10 had. Plus, you can choose the layout you want to start setting up right from the beginning, either by dragging a window to the top of the screen or hovering the mouse over the Restore button.

I still wish it was more like FancyZones, but Snap Layouts was a marked improvement from Windows 10's window management.

4 reasons why I ditched Snap Assist for FancyZones

With FancyZones, I got more layout options and better multitasking support than Snap Assist ever offered.

1
By  Judy Sanhz

It was big for remote work

Remember this one?

Now, this one isn't part of PowerToys anymore, but for a while there, it was one of the best parts of it, and I'm still upset it was removed. Video Conference Mute was a tool that easily let you disable your camera or microphone (or both) using a quick keyboard shortcut, so no matter what video calling software you used, you could always control your camera without remembering different hotkeys or the locations of certain buttons.

As you can probably guess, this was a pretty big deal during the remote work mandates that happened in 2020 and 2021, but the need for something like this became so big that Microsoft built a mute button right into the taskbar some time later, along with a keyboard shortcut, Windows + Alt + K. However, this only worked with certain "supported apps", which, as far as I can tell, only ever included Microsoft Teams.

Despite that, as remote work became less of a requirement, Microsoft eventually removed Video Conference Mute from PowerToys, which is unfortunate since the native integration is far less useful.

My old phone became a $200 webcam upgrade, and the setup took 10 minutes

So many ways to connect those good cameras to your PC reliably.

By  Chandraveer Mathur

Snipping Tool's text extraction also started here

Yes, it was born from PowerToys

Now here we have one where PowerToys was clearly the starting point, but the native Windows equivalent is actually a good bit better. Text Extractor was a PowerToys utility added with version 0.62, all the way back in September 2022, and it worked sort of similarly to the Snipping Tool, where you can select any area of your screen too capture its content. But instead of just grabbing an image, Text Extractor copied the text in that area to your clipboard using optical character recognition (OCR).

One year later, Microsoft started rolling out an update for the built-in Snipping Tool on Windows 11 that added this feature for Windows Insiders, though it was made a lot better here. Instead of automatically copying the text, Snipping Tool simply highlights it, allowing you to select the portions you want to copy, while also giving you the ability to redact text. This meant that not only can you manipulate the text itself, but you can easily modify the original image to black out any sensitive information like phone numbers or email addresses. Later on, Microsoft even added an auto-redaction feature that detects this kind of information and automatically hides it in the image.

The implementation of this feature in Snipping Tool made it even more ubiquitous, and it was so good that PowerToys itself now recommends using that instead. It's one of the best examples of PowerToys being the breeding ground for a fantastic Windows feature.

7 ways Snipping Tool has become better in Windows 11

The Snipping Tool is indispensable now

1
By  João Carrasqueira

Now even the Run dialog is benefiting

Command Palette be praised

The most recent example of PowerToys eventually bringing its benefits to Windows itself has to be the recent update to the Run dialog in Insiders builds of Windows. In Microsoft's words, the new Run dialog (opened with Windows + R) is now powered by the same logic that Command Palette uses, so it loads even faster, at 94ms version 103ms before. Plus, it has a more modernized look and feel, too.

Granted, the speed is far from encompassing everything that makes Command Palette great, but it's something. And it's still a great example of PowerToys bleeding over to Windows 11 to make it better.

There's room for more

Windows 11 could still benefit so much from PowerToys

The above are great examples of how Windows 11 has borrowed features from PowerToys to massively improve itself, but they're also just the tip of the iceberg of how much better Windows could get. PowerToys still has a ton of features that I consider essential for Windows users, but there aren't many signs of most of them coming over to the OS itself.

Deals

Save on PCs, Monitors & Productivity Deals Today

Explore discounts on laptops, monitors, keyboards and productivity accessories — find savings on PCs, docks, ergonomic gear, and software subscriptions to upgrade your work setup and boost productivity.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

FancyZones may have already influenced window management on Windows 11, but frankly, it could be so much better. I use this tool on any computer I use, whether it's Windows, macOS, or Linux, and it should be built into the system. In fact, the KDE Plasma desktop for Linux has a feature like this that's much closer to what I think should be included in Windows 11.

Then there are recent additions like Power Display for managing external monitor brightness, or Light Switch for automatically switching between light and dark themes at specific times of the day. Command Palette as a whole could probably be brought to Windows 11, too, as could some of the benefits of PowerRename or Image Resizer. There are so many things Windows could still take from PowerToys to become the ultimate version of itself.

8 reasons you should be using PowerToys on your Windows PC

Take your productivity to the next level

7
By  João Carrasqueira

At least bundle the app with Windows

I don't expect Microsoft to actually build every PowerToys tool into Windows 11, nor would it make sense to do so considering some of these features have fairly niche use cases. But with how trigger-happy the company is to include more preinstalled junk apps, I'm surprised PowerToys just hasn't been added to that list. It's more useful that 90% of the apps you get with Windows 11, frankly, and you could still uninstall it if you wanted. I'll have to keep my fingers crossed.

Microsoft PowerToys
OS
Windows 10/11
See at Github See at Microsoft Store

item.title: Google's silence on NotebookLM at I/O proves why people don't trust their products
item.itunes:title: Google's silence on NotebookLM at I/O proves why people don't trust their products
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
WWDC is just days away, and despite being a die-hard Apple fan, the tech event I was looking forward to most this year was Google I/O. I knew it was going to be a keynote with the word "AI" mentioned an absurd amount of times. However, I put all that aside because Google's AI efforts have been something I've openly rooted for. But it wasn't AI Overviews, AI Mode in Search, Gemini (or whatever Bard was), or Gemini in Google Maps plus every other Google app, that pulled me in. It was NotebookLM.

Of all the AI products Google has shipped, retired, rebranded, and re-shipped, NotebookLM is the one that felt like a real product made by people who actually used it. I've written an insane number of articles about it, rely on it extensively, and I'm constantly talking about it to anyone that will listen. Naturally, I sat down for the Google I/O keynote with one simple expectation: show me what's next for the one Google AI product I actually love. And then... almost nothing.

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

NotebookLM was only briefly mentioned at I/O 2026

Two hours of AI announcements, and barely a word

While drafting XDA's AI Insider newsletter right after the keynote, I joked about how Google I/O could have easily been called Google A/I (yes, it sounded funnier in my head) because of how many times the word AI was mentioned. So naturally, given NotebookLM was one of their biggest AI products that people obviously love, I patiently waited throughout the keynote to hear something about the tool.

In fact, NotebookLM was first debuted at Google I/O back in 2023 under the name Project Tailwind, and got a proper spotlight at I/O 2025 with the launch of Video Overviews and standalone mobile apps. The precedent was there. And yet, when the keynote wrapped, the most NotebookLM got was a passing mention as the engine behind Literature Insights, a science research tool the company announced at I/O. That was...it.

Ironically enough, Google decided to create a public NotebookLM notebook summarizing everything announced at I/O 2026 to help you catch up on an event where NotebookLM itself was barely worth catching up on.

The reason why the silence was so surprising wasn't just the fact that NotebookLM has been one of Google's most liked AI offerings. Instead, it was that there were also reasons to expect something bigger. In the weeks leading up to I/O, I'd written in my newsletter about leaked features pointing to a pretty significant overhaul including a Canvas option in the Studio panel that would let users turn sources into interactive timelines, webpages, and visualizers, a Connectors feature for pulling in data from other Google services. None of that was announced!

Is NotebookLM becoming just another Gemini tab?

"Complementary" is doing a lot of heavy lifting

You don't need to keep constant tabs on Google to know the direction they like taking their products in. If something works, it eventually gets folded into something bigger. Right now, that something bigger is Gemini, and what happened at Google I/O paired with the moves Google has been making over the last few months has me worried that NotebookLM might be next.

Back in April, Google rolled out a "Notebooks" feature inside the Gemini app. This is positioned as dedicated project spaces where you can organize chats, files, and custom instructions, all syncing bidirectionally with NotebookLM. I covered this feature at XDA back when it launched, and even back then, I mentioned that I wasn't sure I liked the direction NotebookLM seemed to be heading in. I mentioned that Google was drifting too far from everything that made the tool special in the first place. Despite that, I kept hoping I was wrong (and I'm still hoping).

Google will tell you they're complementary — Gemini handles the reasoning, NotebookLM handles grounded research, and it's just two apps sharing one brain. And maybe that's true today. But when a product's signature capabilities start showing up inside the company's flagship AI platform, and then that same product barely gets acknowledged at the company's biggest event of the year, it gets hard not to read the room.

Please stop using NotebookLM as a note-taking app

Stop treating NotebookLM like a...notebook.

5
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Google has a well-documented habit of killing loved products

Could NotebookLM be next?

There's literally a website called Killed by Google dedicated to tracking every product the company has discontinued, and at this point, the list is long enough to make anyone hesitant about getting too attached to a Google product (no matter how good it may be).

Deals

Save on AI tools and subscriptions — top software deals

Discover discounts on software, AI tools, and subscription bundles that boost productivity. Browse offers on productivity apps, cloud services, AI assistants, and supporting tools and accessories to stretch your budget and save more.
Deals Explore Software, AI & Subscriptions Deals

Given that NotebookLM spent months as an experiment in Labs, then graduated from it and became a full-fledged product and is where it is today, I don't really think it'll get killed off. But what I covered in the section above does seem plausible. NotebookLM could stop being its own thing, and just become a tab inside Gemini.

All that said, we might be reading too much into a single keynote. NotebookLM is still getting updates (the team announced Google Drive sync a few days ago), and leaks suggest that exciting features are being worked on. Perhaps the absence from the keynote stage was just a sign that Google had too many other things to show off and NotebookLM didn't make the cut for time.
item.description:
WWDC is just days away, and despite being a die-hard Apple fan, the tech event I was looking forward to most this year was Google I/O. I knew it was going to be a keynote with the word "AI" mentioned an absurd amount of times. However, I put all that aside because Google's AI efforts have been something I've openly rooted for. But it wasn't AI Overviews, AI Mode in Search, Gemini (or whatever Bard was), or Gemini in Google Maps plus every other Google app, that pulled me in. It was NotebookLM.

Of all the AI products Google has shipped, retired, rebranded, and re-shipped, NotebookLM is the one that felt like a real product made by people who actually used it. I've written an insane number of articles about it, rely on it extensively, and I'm constantly talking about it to anyone that will listen. Naturally, I sat down for the Google I/O keynote with one simple expectation: show me what's next for the one Google AI product I actually love. And then... almost nothing.

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

NotebookLM was only briefly mentioned at I/O 2026

Two hours of AI announcements, and barely a word

While drafting XDA's AI Insider newsletter right after the keynote, I joked about how Google I/O could have easily been called Google A/I (yes, it sounded funnier in my head) because of how many times the word AI was mentioned. So naturally, given NotebookLM was one of their biggest AI products that people obviously love, I patiently waited throughout the keynote to hear something about the tool.

In fact, NotebookLM was first debuted at Google I/O back in 2023 under the name Project Tailwind, and got a proper spotlight at I/O 2025 with the launch of Video Overviews and standalone mobile apps. The precedent was there. And yet, when the keynote wrapped, the most NotebookLM got was a passing mention as the engine behind Literature Insights, a science research tool the company announced at I/O. That was...it.

Ironically enough, Google decided to create a public NotebookLM notebook summarizing everything announced at I/O 2026 to help you catch up on an event where NotebookLM itself was barely worth catching up on.

The reason why the silence was so surprising wasn't just the fact that NotebookLM has been one of Google's most liked AI offerings. Instead, it was that there were also reasons to expect something bigger. In the weeks leading up to I/O, I'd written in my newsletter about leaked features pointing to a pretty significant overhaul including a Canvas option in the Studio panel that would let users turn sources into interactive timelines, webpages, and visualizers, a Connectors feature for pulling in data from other Google services. None of that was announced!

Google will tell you they're complementary — Gemini handles the reasoning, NotebookLM handles grounded research, and it's just two apps sharing one brain. And maybe that's true today. But when a product's signature capabilities start showing up inside the company's flagship AI platform, and then that same product barely gets acknowledged at the company's biggest event of the year, it gets hard not to read the room.

Please stop using NotebookLM as a note-taking app

Stop treating NotebookLM like a...notebook.

5
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Google has a well-documented habit of killing loved products

Could NotebookLM be next?

There's literally a website called Killed by Google dedicated to tracking every product the company has discontinued, and at this point, the list is long enough to make anyone hesitant about getting too attached to a Google product (no matter how good it may be).

Perhaps the absence from the keynote stage was just a sign that Google had too many other things to show off and NotebookLM didn't make the cut for time.

item.title: I stopped using motion sensors after my smart home learned to read my PC's state
item.itunes:title: I stopped using motion sensors after my smart home learned to read my PC's state
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Smart home automation room tracking is frustrating. Let's say you place a Zigbee motion sensor in your home office, but because you're sitting quietly working at your PC, reading a long thread, or even just browsing YouTube, the sensor assumes the room is empty and plunges you into darkness. The irony of this is that you're sitting in front of a multi-thousand-dollar machine that knows exactly what you're doing down to the millisecond.

The smartest sensor in your home isn't a dedicated IoT gadget. It's your desktop PC. By exposing your operating system's internal states locally to Home Assistant, your house stops guessing where you are and starts matching its behavior to your immediate digital context. It's time to stop treating your PC as a separate island. By treating your OS as a rich multi-sensor device, you can create hyper-intelligent, context-aware automations that completely eliminate traditional smart home annoyances like lights turning off while you're focused on a screen.

I deployed a network scanner that feeds my LAN statistics into Home Assistant

It's perfect for building Home Assistant automations using LAN data

3
By  Ayush Pande

There are a range of benefits

Your PC is a collection of sensors waiting to be used

Something to keep in mind is that when you're following this process, you have to ditch the traditional cloud route by ruling out clumsy webhooks or cloud software. You're ensuring that this is an instantaneous, offline, privacy-first setup; you don't have to fumble about with any cloud reliance whatsoever. This will not only ensure that even if your internet goes down, the surrounding room won't suddenly get dark, but also means that you can feel safe knowing that hackers can't access your smart home devices.

There are quite a few tools that you can use to do this, and by linking some of them together, you can create automation that you only would have dreamed of in the past. Technical enablers like the HASS workstation service or the IoT Link for Windows are great options. These background services use the Lightweight MQTT, otherwise known as the Message Queuing Telemetry Transport protocol. This means they ping your local MQTT broker (like Mosquitto running inside Home Assistant) with state updates every few seconds. This consumes virtually zero CPU overhead, so you don't have to worry about your PC being overloaded with these background processes.

There is a range of different automations you can use once Home Assistant can read your PC metrics, right down to hyper-granular options. For example, the first can be an in-meeting rule. Your webcam or microphone state changes to on, signaling a Zoom, Discord, or Teams call. Hobo Assistant can trigger a do-not-disturb scene. It automatically changes the LED strip outside your office door to solid red so your family knows not to walk in, and it can also lower the volume of nearby smart speakers to ensure you're not interrupted.

Another can be the immersion rule, which can track your GPU/Active Processes. If your active window matches a game executable or your GPU power draw exceeds 150W, Home Assistant recognizes you are gaming. As a result, it lowers the motorized window blinds to eliminate glare and switches your Philips Hue bias lighting to an immersive preset that matches the title you are in.

An intelligent light is also a great option. The exact second you step away from your desk and hit Win + L to lock your workstation, the office lights are gracefully faded out. No motion sensor cool-down delays required at all.

How to set up your workstation

Simple but effective process

Setting up a HASS workstation service locally is not hard whatsoever. There are a few steps you have to follow to ensure you've done everything correctly, and then you'll be ready to go. The first thing you need to do is verify your MQTT broker. Ensure the Mosquitto broker add-on is running locally on your Home Assistant hub and that you have a dedicated MQTT local user account configured. This will take you just a few minutes to set up if you haven't already done so, and it is an essential part of preparing before installing any services.

Next, you have to install the background service itself. Download the lightweight HASS Workstation Service Installer on your PC. Make sure you run it as an administrator so it can hook cleanly into Windows System Events as a background utility. If you don't do this, you might find yourself being flooded with admin permissions later down the line, and the application not working as intended.

After this, configure the MQTT credentials. Open the user interface app and enter your Home Assistant server, a local IP address, the default port (1883), and your MQTT user credentials. Click Save to establish the secure online link and ensure this is done before proceeding.

And lastly, expose the selected OS states that you want. Toggle on the exact telemetry paths that you want to share. You have a range to choose from based on the different peripherals you use with your PC. For example, you can add webcam active, microphone active, is locked, and active window, as well as so many others. Home Assistant will instantly auto-discover these states as native binary entities. And with that, you're ready to go. Obviously, you need to set up the automations you want to use by implementing these states, but this depends on which automations you actually want to use and your preferences.

The benefit of privacy

Completely local, all the time

The golden rule of this setup is that you have complete and utter privacy and sovereignty over it. For those privacy-conscious readers, knowing that this data path never touches an external server. Your exact active application paths, working hours, and microphone toggles stay 100% contained within your local area network (LAN). You've set up a local boundary that you never have to worry about being accessed by anyone else.

Deals

Smart Home Deals: Save on Gadgets & Automation Gear

Unlock discounts on smart home gadgets and accessories, save on smart bulbs, LED bias lighting, motorized blinds, smart speakers, hubs, and sensors that work with Home Assistant. Browse offers to outfit a privacy-first, PC-aware automation setup.
Deals Explore Smart Home & Gadgets Deals

Another benefit of this system is resource efficiency. Unlike bloated overlay software, these background MQTT services sit quietly in the taskbar, putting less than 15MB of RAM and 0% CPU cycles, keeping your hardware optimized for raw gaming or rendering workloads.

No more buggy sensors

Your smart home should work for you

True home automation shouldn't force you to change your habits to accommodate dumb sensors. It should leverage the rich computational data streams your devices are already producing. Stop buying additional standalone IoT sensors to fix room-tracking bugs. Spend 20 minutes installing a local service wrapper on your rig tonight. Turn your PC into an architectural smart home anchor and finally experience automation that actually understands what you're doing.

Home Assistant
OS
Windows, macOS, Linux
iOS compatible
Yes
Android compatible
Yes
See at Apple App Store See at Google Play Store See at Home Assistant

Another benefit of this system is resource efficiency. Unlike bloated overlay software, these background MQTT services sit quietly in the taskbar, putting less than 15MB of RAM and 0% CPU cycles, keeping your hardware optimized for raw gaming or rendering workloads.

No more buggy sensors

Your smart home should work for you

True home automation shouldn't force you to change your habits to accommodate dumb sensors. It should leverage the rich computational data streams your devices are already producing. Stop buying additional standalone IoT sensors to fix room-tracking bugs. Spend 20 minutes installing a local service wrapper on your rig tonight. Turn your PC into an architectural smart home anchor and finally experience automation that actually understands what you're doing.

item.title: NotebookLM is great until your research gets messy, and that's where Claude Projects wins
item.itunes:title: NotebookLM is great until your research gets messy, and that's where Claude Projects wins
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
NotebookLM has been one of the most reliable tools in my stack for over a year now. Like most of you probably, it's where I go when I've got a pile of, well, anything to make sense of. However, reliability and versatility aren't the same things. NotebookLM is built around a fairly rigid idea of how research should go - you bring it a clean set of sources and it works strictly within them. When you veer off that path the sessions can start to get frustrating pretty quickly.

The problem is, most of my research doesn't start clean. It's usually a whole mess of tabs and pasted text and documents and images, which I haven't sorted into anything yet, or even read through completely yet. This is precisely the kind of input NotebookLM isn't very forgiving of, your results will almost always be subpar when you start a session like this. So I figured I'd need some separate tool to wrangle that early chaos before NotebookLM could do its thing. Turns out my favorite chatbot already had it handled: Claude's Projects. They just happen to be good at the part that NotebookLM keeps making harder than it needs to be.

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

NotebookLM is brilliant, but on its own terms

It's only as tidy as you are

The thing NotebookLM nails, and the reason I keep going back, is how tightly it stays glued to the sources I hand it - you probably don't even need to be told this because we all know that's the whole point of NotebookLM. What I'm getting at is that it's one of the most reliable chat experiences you can have in the sea of AI tools, because it has a very low chance of confidently invented nonsense that regular chatbots tend to produce. Format barely matters either - a dense PDF or a YouTube lecture both get parsed cleanly.

It's also been getting more capable rather than coasting. Deep Research goes off on its own now, digs through the web, and drops a cited report straight into the notebook. And the Gemini tie-in lets me mount a notebook as a source mid-chat, which finally did away with that walled-off feeling around notebooks.

All of that is the benefit you get from NotebookLM, but depending on how you work, it can also be its biggest downside. This process assumes you've already done the sorting, the whole model depends on it: decide what counts as a source, add it, name it, and toggle it on or off. So there's real setup before it's of any use. I build the notebook and curate a clean set first, basically needing the research figured out before I've started, so it doesn't really allow me to have a messy start.

The engineer behind NotebookLM's best features showed me his exact setup, and it changes everything

198 articles later, I finally got to ask the team how they actually use it.

3
By  Mahnoor Faisal

You can show up messy to Claude's Project

Nothing has to warm its place first

Projects is Claude's version of a persistent workspace. It gathers your related chats and uploaded files under a single set of custom instructions, all sharing one context that carries across every conversation in the project. So Claude walks in already knowing what I'm working on before I've typed anything. Setting one up comes down to two pieces: the instructions, where I lay out the background and how I want it to respond, and the knowledge base, where I drop my files (which can either contain further instructions, but I prefer using them for reference points).

It's not too dissimilar from NotebookLM. Claude uses RAG to pull from the knowledge base as needed and doesn't just stuff everything into context at once. The key difference is that it has outside context, web access, and better reasoning, which basically just means that it gets you, and therefore you get to be more messy. While an organized project will be smoother to navigate, it doesn't need to be; I can paste in something I haven't read yet, or a note that contradicts the one right above it, and none of it has to qualify as a proper source before it's of any use to me. This use case might sound silly, but basically I can be a total disaster on my end and still get something useful back.

You don't have to show up prepared: projects is the place you go to to prepare to become prepared.

I tested Gemini Notebooks and Claude Projects side by side, and one didn't make the cut

Same idea, different answers

3
By  Nolen Jonker

Using Claude's projects to turn a junk drawer into a source shortlist

The pre-sort NotebookLM demands

It's tempting to create separate projects for this, but I actually keep it confined to one "junk drawer" project. So you don't even need a paid plan to utilize this method because it won't exceed the 5-project cap for free users. It's the one project where everything lands before I've worked out what it even is, and the instructions are actually the whole trick. I tell Claude up front that this is unsorted stuff, that I've likely not read half of it, might never use some of it, and that the files aren't related to each other unless I say so, since they come from totally different corners of my work.

Here's a version of what to add to the instructions:

This project is a holding pen for unsorted research. Treat every file as raw and independent - I haven't read most of it, I may never use some of it, and items here are NOT related to each other unless I say so. They span different topics and projects. When I ask, help me figure out what a set of these sources is good for, what's worth keeping, and what's redundant. Don't assume context across files. Use web search to sanity-check whether a source actually holds up for what I'm after, and pull in new relevant ones where there's a gap I haven't filled.

The one snag is there's no URL field. A link dropped into the knowledge base just sits there as dead text. Claude may or may not fetch the link, so there's no guarantee that your web page source will be utilized in the project. My workaround is simple: right-click on the page -> print -> save as PDF, then upload the pdf file to the project's knowledge base. It's probably clear by now that I'd love for Anthropic to add a URL field to the project knowledge base already.

Then within that project, I actually use it to circle back to NotebookLM and plan my notebook. I'll tell Claude the exact one I'm about to build, say low-fi wireframing for UX, and what I want out of it, then ask which sources are worth pulling for that goal and why each earns its spot. That "why" is the part NotebookLM can't give me because it won't reason across stuff that isn't sorted. What Claude gives me back is a ready-made shortlist, and I'll actually copy its reasoning straight into the fresh notebook in a note so it opens with a map of what's inside.

My tip, though, is to keep the ask sharp. "What's useful here?" gets you mush. Instead, "which of these explain wireframe fidelity levels for a beginner?" will get you a useful shortlist. Also, I'll ask Claude for clickable links so they open in my browser, and from there I use the NotebookLM Tools extension to add all the open tabs to a notebook in one swoop. So by the time anything reaches NotebookLM, it's already curated, and the curating happened in the one place I get to be a mess.

A place to be disorganized

NotebookLM still does the thing it's best at, but it was never built for the messy front end of research. Claude's projects takes the unsorted pile off my hands and hands back something a notebook can actually use.

Claude
See at Claude
NotebookLM
See at NotebookLM
