item.title: I wasted half a spool of filament before I touched the one slicer setting that fixed everything
item.itunes:title: I wasted half a spool of filament before I touched the one slicer setting that fixed everything
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I wanted the problem to be almost anything except the obvious one. My 3D printer was tuned well enough, my bed was clean, my filament was dry, and my slicer profile looked respectable at a glance. Yet print after print came out with ugly walls, rough top surfaces, and seams that looked more like damage than a normal part of the process. I kept changing everything around the failure instead of admitting that my extrusion settings might be wrong.

Sometimes the printer doesn’t need a rescue mission; it just needs a better idea of how much filament to push.

That’s how I managed to burn through an embarrassing amount of filament before I finally touched flow rate calibration. It wasn’t the most exciting slicer setting, and it definitely didn’t feel like the first place to look when prints were technically completing. But once I calibrated it, the problems I had been chasing separately began to collapse into a single cause. My printer hadn’t been broken, and my filament hadn’t been cursed; I had just been pushing out more plastic than the model actually needed.

7 common 3D printing mistakes I learned to avoid

3D printing beginners often make the same mistakes, but I've got tips on how to avoid them

By  Jeff Butts

Flow rate was quietly ruining prints that looked almost right

The bad prints were finished, but never really clean

The frustrating part was that my prints weren’t failing dramatically. They weren’t peeling off the bed, spaghettiing across the plate, or making the kind of noise that sends you sprinting across the room. Most of them finished, which made the problem easier to ignore and harder to diagnose. A print that completes can still be wrong in ways that waste just as much time.

The first giveaway was the surface texture. Walls had a slightly swollen look, especially around corners and small details, and flat top layers had ridges that caught the light in all the wrong ways. Holes were tighter than they should’ve been, moving parts needed sanding, and support interfaces fused more than I expected. I blamed the model first, then the filament, then the slicer profile as a whole.

That was my mistake. Flow rate isn’t flashy, but it affects nearly every visible part of a print. If the slicer thinks it needs to push more filament than the hotend, nozzle, and material combination actually requires, that extra plastic still has to go somewhere. It gets squeezed into corners, dragged across top layers, and packed into gaps that were supposed to stay clean.

One small percentage change made every other setting behave

My slicer profile stopped fighting the printer at every layer

Once I finally ran a proper flow calibration, the fix wasn’t dramatic on paper. I wasn’t changing temperatures by 20 degrees or replacing hardware. I was adjusting a percentage, which felt too small to explain the mess I had been seeing. That’s probably why I avoided it for so long.

Calibrate the flow rate before you start chasing slicer problems by adjusting speed, temperature, or support. A few percent of over-extrusion can make the top layers rough, holes too tight, corners swollen, and supports harder to remove. Once the flow is dialed in, the rest of your slicer tweaks are much easier to trust.

But small slicer changes can have a large effect when they apply to every single extrusion line. A few percent too much material doesn’t sound serious until it appears across thousands of passes. It changes the way walls stack, how top layers close, and how tight the nozzle feels as it moves through already printed material. Once I brought the flow rate down, the printer immediately sounded less strained, and the finished parts looked less overworked.

The biggest improvement showed up in the areas I had been trying to fix with unrelated settings. Top surfaces became smoother without weirdly lowering speed. Corners looked cleaner without me obsessing over pressure advance first. Supports also started releasing more predictably, because the interface layers weren’t being mashed into the part with too much material behind them.

The usual fixes distracted me from the real problem

I kept tuning around symptoms instead of fixing extrusion

Before touching flow rate, I tried the standard comfort settings. I lowered the print speed, changed the nozzle temperature, adjusted the cooling, and tweaked the seam placement. Some of those changes helped a little, but none of them made the printer feel trustworthy. They just moved the problem around the model.

Lowering the speed made the prints look slightly more controlled, but it didn’t solve the swollen edges. Dropping the temperature reduced some stringing, but it also made certain layers look less consistent. Increasing cooling helped overhangs, yet it didn’t fix the rough top surfaces that kept bothering me. Each change gave me enough improvement to keep experimenting, which was the dangerous part.

That’s where flow rate calibration is easy to underestimate. It doesn’t feel as intuitive as changing temperature or speed, because the printer already looks like it’s extruding normally. Filament is coming out, the layers are sticking, and the model is finishing. But “enough plastic to complete the print” and “the right amount of plastic for accurate extrusion” are not the same thing.

Flow rate calibration still is not a magic repair button

Some print problems really do come from somewhere else

It would be too neat to say flow rate fixes everything. It doesn’t. A wet spool will still string and bubble, a dirty bed will still cause adhesion problems, and a bad model can still produce a miserable print, no matter how clean the slicer profile looks. Flow calibration is important, but it isn’t a universal pardon for every bad habit in a 3D printing workflow.

There are also cases where the flow rate can hide other problems if you treat it too casually. If your nozzle is partially clogged, lowering flow may make a test look better while the actual issue stays lodged in the hotend. If your filament diameter is inconsistent, one calibration print won’t magically make the whole spool behave. Even a worn nozzle can make extrusion look strange in ways that a slicer percentage can’t fully correct.

That’s why I don’t think flow rate should be treated as the only tuning step that matters. It belongs near the beginning of troubleshooting, not at the end after you’ve wasted a pile of prints. You still need to check the basics before you start blaming slicer math. But once those basics are sound, ignoring flow rate turns troubleshooting into busywork.

That limitation is exactly why the setting matters so much

Flow rate gives every other calibration a fair chance

The reason flow rate deserves attention isn’t that it replaces the rest of the tuning process. It matters because it provides a cleaner baseline for the rest of the tuning process. Temperature towers, pressure advance tests, retraction tuning, and support tweaks all become less trustworthy when the printer is already over-extruding or under-extruding. You can still learn something from those tests, but you’re learning through a dirty lens.

After calibrating the flow, the other slicer settings started making more sense. Temperature changes produced clearer results, support settings behaved more predictably, and surface issues stopped blending into one ugly category. That made troubleshooting faster because I wasn’t trying to fix five symptoms caused by one bad assumption. The slicer profile finally felt like something I could tune instead of something I had to wrestle into submission.

It also made me less eager to blame hardware. Before this, I was ready to start inspecting belts, swapping nozzles, and rebuilding parts of a profile that probably didn’t need that much surgery. None of those checks is wrong, but they’re easy to overuse when the real issue is buried in a basic setting. Sometimes the printer doesn’t need a rescue mission; it just needs a better idea of how much filament to push.

The boring slicer setting saved more than filament

I wish I had calibrated the flow rate before wasting half a spool, but the lesson was useful because it was expensive enough to stick. The setting felt boring until I saw how much chaos it had been creating in quiet, repeatable ways. It affected the surfaces, tolerances, seams, supports, and my overall confidence in the printer. Once it was right, the machine didn’t become perfect, but it became predictable again.

That’s the real win with flow calibration. It doesn’t make 3D printing effortless, and it won’t protect you from bad filament, bad models, or bad judgment. But it does remove a major source of hidden noise from the process. If prints look almost right but never clean, flow rate is the slicer setting I’d check before sacrificing another spool on troubleshooting.

OrcaSlicer

OrcaSlicer offers numerous calibration tests you can use to fine-tune your 3D printer, including flow rate calibration.

See at Github

That’s how I managed to burn through an embarrassing amount of filament before I finally touched flow rate calibration. It wasn’t the most exciting slicer setting, and it definitely didn’t feel like the first place to look when prints were technically completing. But once I calibrated it, the problems I had been chasing separately began to collapse into a single cause. My printer hadn’t been broken, and my filament hadn’t been cursed; I had just been pushing out more plastic than the model actually needed.

7 common 3D printing mistakes I learned to avoid

3D printing beginners often make the same mistakes, but I've got tips on how to avoid them

By  Jeff Butts

Flow rate was quietly ruining prints that looked almost right

The bad prints were finished, but never really clean

The frustrating part was that my prints weren’t failing dramatically. They weren’t peeling off the bed, spaghettiing across the plate, or making the kind of noise that sends you sprinting across the room. Most of them finished, which made the problem easier to ignore and harder to diagnose. A print that completes can still be wrong in ways that waste just as much time.

The first giveaway was the surface texture. Walls had a slightly swollen look, especially around corners and small details, and flat top layers had ridges that caught the light in all the wrong ways. Holes were tighter than they should’ve been, moving parts needed sanding, and support interfaces fused more than I expected. I blamed the model first, then the filament, then the slicer profile as a whole.

That was my mistake. Flow rate isn’t flashy, but it affects nearly every visible part of a print. If the slicer thinks it needs to push more filament than the hotend, nozzle, and material combination actually requires, that extra plastic still has to go somewhere. It gets squeezed into corners, dragged across top layers, and packed into gaps that were supposed to stay clean.

One small percentage change made every other setting behave

My slicer profile stopped fighting the printer at every layer

Once I finally ran a proper flow calibration, the fix wasn’t dramatic on paper. I wasn’t changing temperatures by 20 degrees or replacing hardware. I was adjusting a percentage, which felt too small to explain the mess I had been seeing. That’s probably why I avoided it for so long.

Calibrate the flow rate before you start chasing slicer problems by adjusting speed, temperature, or support. A few percent of over-extrusion can make the top layers rough, holes too tight, corners swollen, and supports harder to remove. Once the flow is dialed in, the rest of your slicer tweaks are much easier to trust.

But small slicer changes can have a large effect when they apply to every single extrusion line. A few percent too much material doesn’t sound serious until it appears across thousands of passes. It changes the way walls stack, how top layers close, and how tight the nozzle feels as it moves through already printed material. Once I brought the flow rate down, the printer immediately sounded less strained, and the finished parts looked less overworked.

The biggest improvement showed up in the areas I had been trying to fix with unrelated settings. Top surfaces became smoother without weirdly lowering speed. Corners looked cleaner without me obsessing over pressure advance first. Supports also started releasing more predictably, because the interface layers weren’t being mashed into the part with too much material behind them.

The usual fixes distracted me from the real problem

I kept tuning around symptoms instead of fixing extrusion

Before touching flow rate, I tried the standard comfort settings. I lowered the print speed, changed the nozzle temperature, adjusted the cooling, and tweaked the seam placement. Some of those changes helped a little, but none of them made the printer feel trustworthy. They just moved the problem around the model.

Lowering the speed made the prints look slightly more controlled, but it didn’t solve the swollen edges. Dropping the temperature reduced some stringing, but it also made certain layers look less consistent. Increasing cooling helped overhangs, yet it didn’t fix the rough top surfaces that kept bothering me. Each change gave me enough improvement to keep experimenting, which was the dangerous part.

That’s where flow rate calibration is easy to underestimate. It doesn’t feel as intuitive as changing temperature or speed, because the printer already looks like it’s extruding normally. Filament is coming out, the layers are sticking, and the model is finishing. But “enough plastic to complete the print” and “the right amount of plastic for accurate extrusion” are not the same thing.

Flow rate calibration still is not a magic repair button

Some print problems really do come from somewhere else

It would be too neat to say flow rate fixes everything. It doesn’t. A wet spool will still string and bubble, a dirty bed will still cause adhesion problems, and a bad model can still produce a miserable print, no matter how clean the slicer profile looks. Flow calibration is important, but it isn’t a universal pardon for every bad habit in a 3D printing workflow.

There are also cases where the flow rate can hide other problems if you treat it too casually. If your nozzle is partially clogged, lowering flow may make a test look better while the actual issue stays lodged in the hotend. If your filament diameter is inconsistent, one calibration print won’t magically make the whole spool behave. Even a worn nozzle can make extrusion look strange in ways that a slicer percentage can’t fully correct.

That’s why I don’t think flow rate should be treated as the only tuning step that matters. It belongs near the beginning of troubleshooting, not at the end after you’ve wasted a pile of prints. You still need to check the basics before you start blaming slicer math. But once those basics are sound, ignoring flow rate turns troubleshooting into busywork.

That limitation is exactly why the setting matters so much

Flow rate gives every other calibration a fair chance

The reason flow rate deserves attention isn’t that it replaces the rest of the tuning process. It matters because it provides a cleaner baseline for the rest of the tuning process. Temperature towers, pressure advance tests, retraction tuning, and support tweaks all become less trustworthy when the printer is already over-extruding or under-extruding. You can still learn something from those tests, but you’re learning through a dirty lens.

After calibrating the flow, the other slicer settings started making more sense. Temperature changes produced clearer results, support settings behaved more predictably, and surface issues stopped blending into one ugly category. That made troubleshooting faster because I wasn’t trying to fix five symptoms caused by one bad assumption. The slicer profile finally felt like something I could tune instead of something I had to wrestle into submission.

It also made me less eager to blame hardware. Before this, I was ready to start inspecting belts, swapping nozzles, and rebuilding parts of a profile that probably didn’t need that much surgery. None of those checks is wrong, but they’re easy to overuse when the real issue is buried in a basic setting. Sometimes the printer doesn’t need a rescue mission; it just needs a better idea of how much filament to push.

The boring slicer setting saved more than filament

I wish I had calibrated the flow rate before wasting half a spool, but the lesson was useful because it was expensive enough to stick. The setting felt boring until I saw how much chaos it had been creating in quiet, repeatable ways. It affected the surfaces, tolerances, seams, supports, and my overall confidence in the printer. Once it was right, the machine didn’t become perfect, but it became predictable again.

That’s the real win with flow calibration. It doesn’t make 3D printing effortless, and it won’t protect you from bad filament, bad models, or bad judgment. But it does remove a major source of hidden noise from the process. If prints look almost right but never clean, flow rate is the slicer setting I’d check before sacrificing another spool on troubleshooting.

OrcaSlicer

OrcaSlicer offers numerous calibration tests you can use to fine-tune your 3D printer, including flow rate calibration.

See at Github

item.title: 4 Claude Code settings I change before letting it touch a project
item.itunes:title: 4 Claude Code settings I change before letting it touch a project
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Considering how many AI tools and features have launched in the past three years, we've all gotten into the habit of using them all the same way: sending a prompt and hoping for the best. For a lot of AI products, especially conversational chatbots that are meant to generate an answer and nothing more, that's basically fine. The worst case is a reply you don't like, and you try again.

However, for some tools, spending a few minutes configuring settings before you start makes a bigger difference than you'd imagine. That's especially true for coding agents like Claude Code, and as someone who has spent the better part of a year using it (and writing about it), I've settled on a handful of settings I always change first.

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

I always start with a CLAUDE.md file

The fifteen-minute conversation you only have once

Put coding aside for a bit. When you begin a project with a new teammate in real life, you sit them down and walk them through what you're building instead of throwing them at the work and hoping they figure it out as they go. You explain the quirks of the project, what to do and what not to do, what you're working towards, etc. It's a short, fifteen-minute conversation that saves you from a hundred small misunderstandings later. Claude Code, and basically any other AI tool, is no different. If you drop it into a fresh project and give it no context, it'll just make guesses (likely educated guesses if you're starting with some code), but guesses all the same.

A CLAUDE.md file is how you skip that entirely. It's a plain and simple markdown file you drop into your project's root directory, and Claude Code reads it at the start of every session before it does anything else. You write down exactly what it needs to know once, like the tech stack you're using, your folder structure, the conventions you've already settled on, the things you don't want it touching, etc. Claude Code then carries that context into every prompt from then on, and saves you the hassle of correcting it every few messages or starting from scratch every session.

At the start of a project, you can simply run the /init command inside your project's directory, and Claude Code will scan everything, figure out your stack, and generate a starter CLAUDE.md for you. The version it generates won't be perfect and obviously won't be mapped to your preferences, but it's often a good starting point. Whenever Claude Code makes a mistake I wouldn't want it to repeat next time, I also make sure to open the file and add a quick line about it.

Claude Code's creator keeps sharing tips, and they all made my experience better

Who better to learn from than the person who built it?

By  Mahnoor Faisal

Set up permissions before the first prompt

Stop hitting Enter on the same prompts a hundred times a day

If you've used Claude Code for a project, you've likely spent a good amount of time hitting the Enter key to grant Claude Code permission to do something basic. By default, the tool stops and asks you every time it wants to run a bash command, make a file edit, search the web, etc. The intent is to keep you in the loop, but in practice, what actually happens is one of two things. Either you spend half the session clicking "yes" on the same npm install and git status prompts you'd approve a hundred times in a row, or you get fed up, flip on --dangerously-skip-permissions, and hand Claude the keys to your entire machine.

Fortunately, there's a middle path, which is configuring permissions upfront. Claude Code runs on a system of allow, ask, and deny rules that live in a separate .claude/settings.json file. You can manage them through the /permissions command inside Claude Code, or edit the JSON directly if you prefer. So, before I begin a project, I always take a few minutes out to configure the permissions depending on the type of project I'm working on.

Always start in plan mode

Look before you let it leap

Before I let Claude write a single line of code in a new project, I flip it into plan mode. It's one of Claude Code's permission modes, and you can toggle into it (and out of it) by hitting Shift+Tab inside the terminal. In plan mode, Claude can read your files and run read-only shell commands to look around, but it can't edit your code. This is the single best habit I've picked up for starting a project on the right foot, and it's saved me from more bad ideas than I'd like to admit.

Once I've reviewed the plan and I'm happy with it, I Shift+Tab back into a different mode depending on what I'm doing. The default mode is what most people are familiar with, where Claude prompts you the first time it wants to use each tool and then remembers your answer. If I'm deep into a long task and I know I'll be approving every edit anyway, I'll bump it up to acceptEdits, which auto-accepts file edits and common filesystem commands (mkdir, touch, mv, cp) inside my working directory.

There are two other modes worth knowing about, though I use them less. auto mode is a newer one, currently labeled a research preview, that auto-approves tool calls after running background safety checks to verify the actions actually match what you asked for. Then there's bypassPermissions, which, like the name suggests, runs everything without asking you. Anthropic itself recommends running this within isolated containers and VMs only. I don't personally use it on anything that lives on my main machine, and I'd recommend the same. While these aren't settings you configure once, I did want to mention them as part of my pre-project setup because picking the right starting mode shapes the whole session.

I pick the model based on what I'm doing

The right model is whichever one fits the task

Claude Code lets you pick between three models: Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku. Most people set this once during setup and never think about it again. That's fine, but it leaves a lot on the table: you're paying Sonnet time for things Haiku could finish in half the time, and you're missing out on Opus-level reasoning on the projects that actually need it.

Deals

Save on AI tools and subscriptions with top deals

Explore limited-time discounts and special offers across software, AI subscriptions, and developer services—find deals that deliver real savings on coding platforms, cloud credits, plugins, and productivity tools. Shop and compare offers now to lower costs and boost your workflow
Deals Explore Software, AI & Subscriptions Deals

Before I start a project, I just take a second to decide which one fits. Technical work that involves reasoning or anything I expect to debug a lot gets Opus, simple scripts and reorganization tasks get Haiku, and everything in between stays on Sonnet. The clever option, and the one I use most often, is opusplan. Run /model opusplan and Claude Code will use Opus during plan mode (when you actually need the deep thinking) and automatically switch to Sonnet once you start implementing it.

There's also a /effort command that sets the effort level for the whole session, which controls how much "thinking budget" Claude gets before responding. I leave it on the default for most work and bump it up to high or max when I'm tackling something I know is going to need real reasoning!

These are the settings I'd never skip

One of the reasons why Claude Code is a great tool is because of how customizable it is. While I could keep writing about all the little settings and tweaks I've picked up over time, the four above are the ones I'd genuinely never skip on a new project. They take five minutes to set up and pay off every session after, which is about the best return you'll get from any settings menu.

A CLAUDE.md file is how you skip that entirely. It's a plain and simple markdown file you drop into your project's root directory, and Claude Code reads it at the start of every session before it does anything else. You write down exactly what it needs to know once, like the tech stack you're using, your folder structure, the conventions you've already settled on, the things you don't want it touching, etc. Claude Code then carries that context into every prompt from then on, and saves you the hassle of correcting it every few messages or starting from scratch every session.

At the start of a project, you can simply run the /init command inside your project's directory, and Claude Code will scan everything, figure out your stack, and generate a starter CLAUDE.md for you. The version it generates won't be perfect and obviously won't be mapped to your preferences, but it's often a good starting point. Whenever Claude Code makes a mistake I wouldn't want it to repeat next time, I also make sure to open the file and add a quick line about it.

Claude Code's creator keeps sharing tips, and they all made my experience better

Who better to learn from than the person who built it?

By  Mahnoor Faisal

Set up permissions before the first prompt

Stop hitting Enter on the same prompts a hundred times a day

If you've used Claude Code for a project, you've likely spent a good amount of time hitting the Enter key to grant Claude Code permission to do something basic. By default, the tool stops and asks you every time it wants to run a bash command, make a file edit, search the web, etc. The intent is to keep you in the loop, but in practice, what actually happens is one of two things. Either you spend half the session clicking "yes" on the same npm install and git status prompts you'd approve a hundred times in a row, or you get fed up, flip on --dangerously-skip-permissions, and hand Claude the keys to your entire machine.

Fortunately, there's a middle path, which is configuring permissions upfront. Claude Code runs on a system of allow, ask, and deny rules that live in a separate .claude/settings.json file. You can manage them through the /permissions command inside Claude Code, or edit the JSON directly if you prefer. So, before I begin a project, I always take a few minutes out to configure the permissions depending on the type of project I'm working on.

Always start in plan mode

Look before you let it leap

Before I let Claude write a single line of code in a new project, I flip it into plan mode. It's one of Claude Code's permission modes, and you can toggle into it (and out of it) by hitting Shift+Tab inside the terminal. In plan mode, Claude can read your files and run read-only shell commands to look around, but it can't edit your code. This is the single best habit I've picked up for starting a project on the right foot, and it's saved me from more bad ideas than I'd like to admit.

Once I've reviewed the plan and I'm happy with it, I Shift+Tab back into a different mode depending on what I'm doing. The default mode is what most people are familiar with, where Claude prompts you the first time it wants to use each tool and then remembers your answer. If I'm deep into a long task and I know I'll be approving every edit anyway, I'll bump it up to acceptEdits, which auto-accepts file edits and common filesystem commands (mkdir, touch, mv, cp) inside my working directory.

There are two other modes worth knowing about, though I use them less. auto mode is a newer one, currently labeled a research preview, that auto-approves tool calls after running background safety checks to verify the actions actually match what you asked for. Then there's bypassPermissions, which, like the name suggests, runs everything without asking you. Anthropic itself recommends running this within isolated containers and VMs only. I don't personally use it on anything that lives on my main machine, and I'd recommend the same. While these aren't settings you configure once, I did want to mention them as part of my pre-project setup because picking the right starting mode shapes the whole session.

I pick the model based on what I'm doing

The right model is whichever one fits the task

Claude Code lets you pick between three models: Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku. Most people set this once during setup and never think about it again. That's fine, but it leaves a lot on the table: you're paying Sonnet time for things Haiku could finish in half the time, and you're missing out on Opus-level reasoning on the projects that actually need it.

Before I start a project, I just take a second to decide which one fits. Technical work that involves reasoning or anything I expect to debug a lot gets Opus, simple scripts and reorganization tasks get Haiku, and everything in between stays on Sonnet. The clever option, and the one I use most often, is opusplan. Run /model opusplan and Claude Code will use Opus during plan mode (when you actually need the deep thinking) and automatically switch to Sonnet once you start implementing it.

There's also a /effort command that sets the effort level for the whole session, which controls how much "thinking budget" Claude gets before responding. I leave it on the default for most work and bump it up to high or max when I'm tackling something I know is going to need real reasoning!

These are the settings I'd never skip

One of the reasons why Claude Code is a great tool is because of how customizable it is. While I could keep writing about all the little settings and tweaks I've picked up over time, the four above are the ones I'd genuinely never skip on a new project. They take five minutes to set up and pay off every session after, which is about the best return you'll get from any settings menu.
